Task id: T80 (ledgerRefs goals:G4)
Base commit: 553ebc9b4085afc25d3d1204afd477262b543218 (main)
Branch: implement/T80 — your worktree is created by native isolation; FIRST run `git switch -c implement/T80` inside YOUR worktree so your commit lands on the task branch the orchestrator merges.

NOTE: this is a Go repository (wanbond). Wherever your role template says `bun run check`, use this repo's check gate instead (per AGENTS.md): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` from the worktree root. There is no package.json / bun here.

headline: Add per-peer psk and name fields to config.Peer

description: In internal/config, add `psk config.Key` (toml `psk`) and `name string` (toml `name`) to config.Peer. Keep top-level Config.PSK as the single-peer default (unchanged for existing configs). Normalize in normalize()/resolveEndpoints-adjacent code so a single-peer config with only top-level psk keeps its exact current shape. Do NOT touch the edge-side schema beyond the value it already sets. No datapath change in this task.

acceptance: A new config unit test parses a 2-peer TOML with distinct per-peer `psk`+`name` values and a legacy single-peer TOML carrying only top-level `psk`; the single-peer parse is byte-identical to today (golden struct compare), and the multi-peer parse exposes each peer's psk/name. `go test ./internal/config/...` passes.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	package config
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/base64"
5		"errors"
6		"fmt"
7		"math"
8		"net/netip"
9		"strconv"
10		"strings"
11		"time"
12	
13		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
14	)
15	
16	// Role selects which end of the tunnel this process runs as. It is an explicit,
17	// required field; the role is never inferred from other configuration.
18	type Role string
19	
20	const (
21		// RoleEdge is the mobile Linux box that bonds the WAN uplinks.
22		RoleEdge Role = "edge"
23		// RoleConcentrator is the public-IP VPS that terminates the tunnel.
24		RoleConcentrator Role = "concentrator"
25	)
26	
27	func (r Role) valid() bool {
28		return r == RoleEdge || r == RoleConcentrator
29	}
30	
31	// keyLen is the byte length of a Curve25519 WireGuard key and of a raw
32	// outer-control PSK, before base64 encoding.
33	const keyLen = 32
34	
35	// Config is the whole wanbond configuration, shared by both roles and parsed
36	// from a single TOML file.
37	type Config struct {
38		Role      Role            `toml:"role"`
39		Paths     []Path          `toml:"paths"`
40		WireGuard WireGuard       `toml:"wireguard"`
41		Amnezia   Amnezia         `toml:"amnezia"`
42		PSK       Key             `toml:"psk"`
43		Metrics   Metrics         `toml:"metrics"`
44		Log       Log             `toml:"log"`
45		Scheduler SchedulerConfig `toml:"scheduler"`
46		FEC       FEC             `toml:"fec"`
47	}
48	
49	// SchedulerPolicy selects the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind
50	// runs. It is a bounded enum: the P1 active-backup failover (default) or the T21
51	// weighted-aggregation policy. The policy is an explicit config choice — active-
52	// backup stays selectable so the P1 behaviour is never removed, only extended.
53	type SchedulerPolicy string
54	
55	const (
56		// PolicyActiveBackup is the P1 single-active-path failover scheduler: all egress
57		// on the highest-priority live path, every other path idle (data-thrift by
58		// construction). It is the default when [scheduler] is omitted.
59		PolicyActiveBackup SchedulerPolicy = "active-backup"
60		// PolicyWeighted is the T21 weighted-aggregation scheduler: under load a single
61		// flow is striped across both paths in proportion to per-path RTT/loss-derived
62		// weight, collapsing to the primary at low load (5G stays ~idle) with hysteresis,
63		// and per-path send-pacing to bound bufferbloat.
64		PolicyWeighted SchedulerPolicy = "weighted"
65	)
66	
67	func (p SchedulerPolicy) valid() bool {
68		return p == PolicyActiveBackup || p == PolicyWeighted
69	}
70	
71	// Weighted-aggregation policy defaults (T21). They are applied when [scheduler]
72	// selects the weighted policy but leaves a knob at its zero value, so a minimal
73	// `policy = "weighted"` block is usable without hand-tuning every threshold. They
74	// are conservative bring-up values; the empirical per-path pace is sized from a
75	// measured BDP in a bandwidth-capped fixture (P0 findings §7, deferred to T35/T23),
76	// so the shipped default leaves pacing DISABLED and only bounds distribution.
77	const (
78		// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity, in frame-selection
79		// slots per second, that the aggregation load-gate compares offered load against
80		// AND (when pacing is enabled) the per-path token-bucket refill rate. It is a
81		// synthetic proxy for path capacity: there is no measured BDP (P0 §7), so capacity
82		// is expressed in the only unit the scheduler sees — Pick() invocations per second.
83		defaultPerPathCapacityFPS = 10000.0
84		// defaultEngageFraction engages aggregation once offered load exceeds this
85		// fraction of one path's capacity — i.e. a single path is nearly saturated.
86		defaultEngageFraction = 0.9
87		// defaultDisengageFraction collapses back to primary-only once offered load falls
88		// below this fraction of one path's capacity. It is strictly below the engage
89		// fraction: the gap is the hysteresis band that stops the 5G path from dribbling
90		// on/off around a single threshold (requirement 2, data-thrift).
91		defaultDisengageFraction = 0.5
92		// defaultCollapseDwell is how long offered load must stay continuously below the
93		// disengage fraction before aggregation collapses to primary-only — the temporal
94		// half of the hysteresis, mirroring the active-backup failback dwell so a brief
95		// lull does not repeatedly park then re-engage the metered path.
96		defaultCollapseDwell = 2 * time.Second
97		// defaultLoadTau is the time constant of the exponentially-weighted offered-load
98		// rate estimator. It smooths the instantaneous Pick() arrival rate so the gate
99		// reacts to sustained load, not single-frame bursts.
100		defaultLoadTau = 200 * time.Millisecond
101		// defaultPacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst (in frame slots)
102		// when pacing is enabled: the bucket admits up to this many frames instantaneously
103		// before the refill rate binds, absorbing normal jitter without inflating a queue.
104		defaultPacingBurstFrames = 64.0
105		// defaultWeightRTTFloor floors the RTT in the weight formula so a path reporting a
106		// near-zero RTT (cold estimator, no samples yet) cannot be handed unbounded weight.
107		defaultWeightRTTFloor = 1 * time.Millisecond
108		// defaultWeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root so a zero-loss
109		// path gets a large-but-finite weight and two zero-loss paths split by inverse-RTT
110		// alone rather than both diverging.
111		defaultWeightLossFloor = 1e-3
112	)
113	
114	// SchedulerConfig selects and tunes the send scheduler. When the [scheduler] block
115	// is omitted the policy defaults to active-backup and every weighted knob is
116	// ignored, so existing configs keep the P1 behaviour unchanged. The weighted knobs
117	// are validated (and defaulted) only when the weighted policy is selected.
118	type SchedulerConfig struct {
119		// Policy selects the scheduler; defaults to active-backup when empty.
120		Policy SchedulerPolicy `toml:"policy"`

440	
441	// WireGuard holds the inner tunnel's key material.
442	type WireGuard struct {
443		PrivateKey Key    `toml:"private_key"`
444		Peers      []Peer `toml:"peers"`
445		// ListenPort is the UDP port the concentrator listens on; 0 on the edge.
446		ListenPort uint16 `toml:"listen_port"`
447	}
448	
449	// Peer is one WireGuard peer.
450	type Peer struct {
451		PublicKey Key `toml:"public_key"`
452		// Endpoint is the peer's SINGLE tunnel address (edge -> concentrator); the
453		// legacy single-endpoint config form, mutually exclusive with EndpointsRaw.
454		// Empty on the concentrator, which roams the edge's endpoint dynamically.
455		// A bare `endpoint = "..."` is normalized in resolveEndpoints to a one-
456		// element Endpoints list, so it stays behavior-identical to the pre-T54
457		// config surface.
458		Endpoint string `toml:"endpoint"`
459		// EndpointsRaw is the ORDERED list of concentrator (peer) endpoint address:port
460		// strings for the edge-side hub-failover config surface (Q18): index 0 is the
461		// active/primary concentrator, the rest are ordered standbys. Edge-only; a
462		// concentrator declaring it is a config error (validate). Mutually exclusive
463		// with the legacy Endpoint field.
464		EndpointsRaw []string `toml:"endpoints"`
465		// Endpoints is EndpointsRaw (or the single legacy Endpoint, as a one-element

477	// Endpoints list (Q18): the legacy single Endpoint field and the new ordered
478	// EndpointsRaw list are mutually exclusive input forms, but a bare `endpoint =
479	// "..."` is normalized here to a ONE-ELEMENT Endpoints list — so it stays

483	// runtime, and duplicates within the list are rejected.
484	func (p *Peer) resolveEndpoints() error {
485		if p.Endpoint != "" && len(p.EndpointsRaw) > 0 {
486			return errors.New("endpoint and endpoints are mutually exclusive; endpoint is the single-entry legacy form of endpoints")
487		}
488		raw := p.EndpointsRaw
489		if p.Endpoint != "" {
490			raw = []string{p.Endpoint}
491		}
492		seen := make(map[netip.AddrPort]struct{}, len(raw))
493		endpoints := make([]netip.AddrPort, 0, len(raw))
494		for _, s := range raw {

498			}
499			if _, dup := seen[ap]; dup {

501			}
502			seen[ap] = struct{}{}

504		}

118	type SchedulerConfig struct {
119		// Policy selects the scheduler; defaults to active-backup when empty.
120		Policy SchedulerPolicy `toml:"policy"`
121	
122		// PerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity in frame-selection slots
123		// per second: the denominator the aggregation load-gate compares offered load
124		// against, and the per-path pacing refill rate when PacingEnabled. Must be > 0
125		// under the weighted policy.
126		PerPathCapacityFPS float64 `toml:"per_path_capacity_fps"`
127		// EngageFraction engages aggregation when offered load exceeds
128		// EngageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS. Must be in (0,1].
129		EngageFraction float64 `toml:"engage_fraction"`
130		// DisengageFraction collapses aggregation to primary-only once offered load stays
131		// below DisengageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS for CollapseDwell. Must be in
132		// [0,EngageFraction) — strictly below EngageFraction so the two form a hysteresis
133		// band.
134		DisengageFraction float64 `toml:"disengage_fraction"`
135		// CollapseDwell is the sustained-low-load dwell before collapsing to primary-only
136		// (hysteresis). Must be >= 0.
137		CollapseDwell time.Duration `toml:"collapse_dwell"`
138		// LoadTau is the offered-load rate estimator's time constant. Must be > 0.
139		LoadTau time.Duration `toml:"load_tau"`
140	
141		// PacingEnabled turns per-path send-pacing on. When false the token buckets are
142		// bypassed (a documented no-op — P0 §7 could not empirically size the pace in the
143		// unmetered fixture), but PerPathCapacityFPS still drives the aggregation gate.
144		PacingEnabled bool `toml:"pacing_enabled"`
145		// PacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst in frame slots. Must be > 0
146		// when PacingEnabled.
147		PacingBurstFrames float64 `toml:"pacing_burst_frames"`

149		// WeightRTTFloor floors RTT in the weight formula (must be > 0 under weighted).
150		WeightRTTFloor time.Duration `toml:"weight_rtt_floor"`
151		// WeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root (must be > 0 under
152		// weighted).
153		WeightLossFloor float64 `toml:"weight_loss_floor"`
154	}

156	// BDPSizing is a per-path pacing sizing derived from a MEASURED path, in the
157	// frame-selection-slot units the weighted scheduler pace uses (defect D22). It is the
158	// empirical alternative to the synthetic frame-count defaults: CapacityFPS replaces
159	// PerPathCapacityFPS and BurstFrames replaces PacingBurstFrames.
160	type BDPSizing struct {
161		// CapacityFPS is the sustained per-path frame rate the measured bottleneck bandwidth
162		// supports (the token-bucket refill rate and aggregation-gate denominator).
163		CapacityFPS float64
164		// BurstFrames is the bandwidth-delay product expressed in frames (bandwidth x RTT,
165		// i.e. one RTT of in-flight frames): the token-bucket burst that absorbs one RTT of
166		// jitter without building a standing queue.
167		BurstFrames float64
168	}
169	
170	// defaultAvgWireFrameBytes is the conservative average on-wire outer-frame size used
171	// when deriving the per-path pace from an operator-declared bandwidth at config load
172	// (T53): a full IPv4 path MTU, since a full-MTU DATA datagram occupies about one path
173	// MTU on the wire. It mirrors bind.DefaultPathMTU (1500) rather than importing it —
174	// internal/bind imports internal/config, so config cannot import bind without a cycle
175	// (the same mirror-with-cross-reference pattern as maxFECDeadline above). Sizing
176	// capacity with the full-MTU frame is the conservative floor: smaller average frames
177	// would yield a HIGHER frame rate, so this never over-paces a path.
178	const defaultAvgWireFrameBytes = 1500.0
179	
180	// SizePacingFromBDP derives the weighted scheduler's per-path pacing parameters from a
181	// measured path instead of the synthetic frame-count default (defect D22). The shipped
182	// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS (10000, ~115 Mbit/s at full MTU) sits far above a realistic
183	// slow uplink, so the aggregation gate may never engage and — with pacing enabled — the
184	// pace never binds; deriving capacity from the measured bottleneck bandwidth fixes both.
185	//
186	// capacity_fps = bandwidth / (8 * avg wire frame bytes) — frames/s the link sustains.
187	// burst_frames = capacity_fps * RTT — one RTT of in-flight frames (equivalently
188	// BDP_bytes / avg wire frame bytes, since the bandwidth-delay product BDP = bandwidth * RTT).
189	//
190	// bandwidthBitsPerSec is the measured bottleneck bandwidth (bits/s), rtt the measured
191	// path RTT, and avgWireFrameBytes the average on-wire outer-frame size (a full-MTU
192	// datagram plus frame.DataOverhead is the conservative choice). It fails fast on a
193	// non-positive input rather than emitting a nonsensical (zero/negative/Inf) sizing.
194	func SizePacingFromBDP(bandwidthBitsPerSec float64, rtt time.Duration, avgWireFrameBytes float64) (BDPSizing, error) {
195		if bandwidthBitsPerSec <= 0 {
196			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing bandwidth must be > 0 bit/s, got %g", bandwidthBitsPerSec)
197		}
198		if rtt <= 0 {
199			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing RTT must be > 0, got %s", rtt)
200		}
201		if avgWireFrameBytes <= 0 {
202			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing average wire frame size must be > 0 bytes, got %g", avgWireFrameBytes)
203		}
204		const bitsPerByte = 8.0
205		capacityFPS := bandwidthBitsPerSec / (bitsPerByte * avgWireFrameBytes)
206		burstFrames := capacityFPS * rtt.Seconds()
207		return BDPSizing{CapacityFPS: capacityFPS, BurstFrames: burstFrames}, nil
208	}
209	
210	// bandwidthUnit is a recognised link_bandwidth suffix and its bit/s multiplier.
211	type bandwidthUnit struct {
212		suffix string
213		mult   float64
214	}
215	
216	// bandwidthUnits are the accepted operator-facing bandwidth suffixes, longest-first so
217	// "gbit" is matched before "bit". SI decimal multipliers (k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9) over a
218	// bit/s base; "bps" is accepted as an alias of "bit" (and "kbps"/"mbps"/"gbps" likewise).
219	var bandwidthUnits = []bandwidthUnit{
220		{"gbit", 1e9}, {"gbps", 1e9},
221		{"mbit", 1e6}, {"mbps", 1e6},
222		{"kbit", 1e3}, {"kbps", 1e3},
223		{"bit", 1}, {"bps", 1},
224	}
225	
226	// parseBandwidth parses an operator-declared link bandwidth such as "50Mbit", "1Gbit",
227	// or "500kbit" into bits per second. It requires an explicit bit/s unit suffix so a
228	// bare unitless number cannot be silently misread, and fails fast on an empty or
229	// otherwise unparseable value — a mistyped bandwidth is rejected at config load.
230	func parseBandwidth(s string) (float64, error) {
231		lower := strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(s))
232		if lower == "" {
233			return 0, errors.New("empty bandwidth")
234		}
235		for _, u := range bandwidthUnits {
236			if !strings.HasSuffix(lower, u.suffix) {
237				continue
238			}
239			num := strings.TrimSpace(lower[:len(lower)-len(u.suffix)])
240			val, err := strconv.ParseFloat(num, 64)
241			if err != nil {
242				return 0, fmt.Errorf("invalid number %q", num)
243			}
244			return val * u.mult, nil
245		}
246		return 0, fmt.Errorf("missing bit/s unit suffix (want e.g. %q)", "50Mbit")
247	}
248	
249	// FEC group-close deadline default (T24). Applied only when [fec] is enabled but
250	// leaves the deadline at its zero value, so a minimal `enabled = true` block emits
251	// parity for a partially-filled group promptly under low load rather than stranding
252	// it until the size threshold fills.
253	const defaultFECDeadline = 5 * time.Millisecond
254	
255	// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor mirrors adaptivefec.DefaultSafetyFactor (1.5), the
256	// simulation-proven controller default, restated here (with this cross-reference rather
257	// than an import) so a minimal `adaptive = true` block gets the proven tuning without
258	// coupling config to the controller package. NOTE: 1.5 sizes M for the mean loss with
259	// modest variance headroom; a tight residual SLA under a specific loss/geometry may need a
260	// higher factor (e.g. at 5% loss with K=10 the 1.5 default sizes M=1, ~1% residual — raise
261	// this to lift M and drive the residual down).
262	const defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor = 1.5
263	
264	// maxFECDeadline bounds the FEC group-close deadline at load time (T24, defect #4). It
265	// MUST stay at or below the multipath Bind's authoritative bound (bind.maxFECDeadline =
266	// resequencerTimeout/2 = 125ms): a group flushed by the deadline emits its parity
267	// `deadline` after opening, and the reconstructed frames must reach the receive
268	// resequencer BEFORE it skips the gap (its 250ms per-gap timeout) — otherwise recovery
269	// is structurally too late (the gap is skipped, the recovered frame dropped as past the
270	// release point) while /metrics would still count it reconstructed. Rejecting an
271	// over-large deadline at load makes that coupling fail-fast and explicit rather than
272	// silently defeating FEC. Kept in lockstep with bind.maxFECDeadline (the packages
273	// cannot import each other, so the value is mirrored with this cross-reference).
274	const maxFECDeadline = 125 * time.Millisecond
275	
276	// maxFECShards mirrors the Reed-Solomon field limit enforced in internal/fec: a
277	// coding group carries at most 256 shards (data + parity) total over GF(2^8). It

279	// locus, with the same bound the codec enforces internally.

288	type FEC struct {
289		// Enabled turns the FEC plane on. When false every other field is ignored and the
290		// datapath carries no parity.
291		Enabled bool `toml:"enabled"`
292		// DataShards is K: the number of inner datagrams grouped before parity is emitted.
293		// Must be >= 1 when enabled.
294		DataShards int `toml:"data_shards"`
295		// ParityShards is M: the parity frames emitted per group and the maximum number of
296		// per-group data losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1 when enabled. In
297		// adaptive mode it is the CEILING (and the receiver's decoder cardinality) — the
298		// controller drives the per-group parity in [0,ParityShards] to track measured loss.
299		ParityShards int `toml:"parity_shards"`
300		// Deadline bounds grouping latency: a partially-filled group is flushed (parity
301		// emitted over its current data frames) once this much time has elapsed since its
302		// first frame. Defaults to defaultFECDeadline when enabled and left zero; must be
303		// > 0 after defaulting.
304		Deadline time.Duration `toml:"deadline"`
305		// Adaptive opts the send-side FEC into the closed-loop controller (T27/T29): the
306		// per-group parity count tracks the measured per-path loss instead of standing at
307		// the fixed ParityShards ratio, so a clean path spends near-zero overhead while a
308		// lossy one is masked. It is OFF by default, so an existing [fec] block keeps the
309		// fixed-ratio behaviour (T24) byte-for-byte. When true, ParityShards is reinterpreted
310		// as the controller's parity ceiling (see above); the receiver is unchanged because a
311		// group coded with fewer parity shards decodes against the ParityShards-ceiling codec
312		// unchanged (klauspost's parity is prefix-consistent).
313		Adaptive bool `toml:"adaptive"`
314		// SafetyFactor is the adaptive controller's headroom multiplier over the measured mean
315		// loss the per-group parity is sized to mask (adaptivefec.Config.SafetyFactor). It is
316		// the residual-loss LEVER: the controller sizes M so M/(K+M) >= SafetyFactor*loss, so a
317		// higher factor spends more parity to keep the post-recovery residual under a tighter
318		// bound against binomial per-group variance. Applies only in adaptive mode; defaults to
319		// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor (the simulation-proven controller default) when left zero
320		// AND target_residual is unset, and must be >= 1. Ignored (and must stay zero) in fixed
321		// mode, and mutually exclusive with target_residual (set one, not both).
322		SafetyFactor float64 `toml:"safety_factor"`
323		// TargetResidual is the adaptive controller's target POST-RECOVERY residual-loss SLA
324		// (a fraction in (0,1)) and the PRIMARY adaptive sizing surface (D26/T46): the
325		// controller derives the per-group parity M by inverting the binomial residual model
326		// E[max(0,D-M)]/K (D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss)) to the smallest M meeting this target,
327		// capped at the parity ceiling (parity_shards). It SUPERSEDES safety_factor — set
328		// EITHER a residual SLA (target_residual, recommended: it maps an operator's loss
329		// budget directly to redundancy) OR the bare headroom multiplier (safety_factor), never
330		// both. Applies only in adaptive mode; unset (0) selects the safety_factor path. Must
331		// be in (0,1) when set. Ignored (and must stay zero) in fixed mode.
332		TargetResidual float64 `toml:"target_residual"`
333	}

335	// applyDefaults fills the group-close deadline when FEC is enabled and the deadline
336	// was left at zero. It is a no-op for a disabled block, so a config that never turns
337	// FEC on keeps an empty [fec] surface.

# wanbond task runner. Targets assume the dev shell is active (`nix develop`),
# which puts go, golangci-lint, and the netem/DPI tooling on PATH.

# Show available targets.
default:
    @just --list

# Build all non-privileged packages.
build:
    go build ./...

# Cross-compile static release binaries (CGO_ENABLED=0, stripped, version
# stamped from git) for linux/amd64 and linux/arm64 into dist/. `file` reports
# them statically linked; see docs/install.md for the deployment steps.
release:
    mkdir -p dist
    CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build -trimpath -ldflags "-s -w -X main.version=$(git describe --tags --always --dirty)" -o dist/wanbond-linux-amd64 ./cmd/wanbond
    CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64 go build -trimpath -ldflags "-s -w -X main.version=$(git describe --tags --always --dirty)" -o dist/wanbond-linux-arm64 ./cmd/wanbond

# The e2e/realhosts sources are gated behind //go:build tags, so a tagless
# vet/lint never compiles them and silently skips them (see D28). The tagged
# passes below are additive — the tagless run still analyses the default build
# exactly as before.
# Vet + lint the default build plus the e2e- and realhosts-tagged sources.
lint:
    go vet ./...
    go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e/...
    go vet -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/...
    golangci-lint run
    golangci-lint run --build-tags e2e ./test/e2e/...
